coredata.dc:title: Triple-emission nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots from lignin: Highly fluorescent sensing platform for detection of hexavalent chromium ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Lingli
Shen, Dekui
Hong Luo, Kai
coredata.dc:description: Considering that hexavalent chromium ions (Cr6+) with high toxicity poses a huge threat to human health and the ecological environment, constructing a rapid and accurate sensing platform is of great significance in detecting the toxic substance. The novel nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, B-CQDs) from lignin are synthesized as fluorescent sensors for the detection of Cr6+. The synthetic processes involve the acid hydrolysis step followed by the hydrothermal treatment step. Lignin is firstly depolymerized by cleaving ether bonds in the acidolysis, and N, B-CQDs are consequently formed by the aromatic re-fusion of lignin nanoparticles in the hydrothermal process. The lignin-derived N, B-CQDs show triple emission of purple, blue and green fluorescence under the excitation of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively. The triple-emission N, B-CQDs are applied for the triple-channel detection of Cr6+, which exhibit highly sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching for Cr6+ with good linearity (R2 ≤ 0.996) and very low limit of detection as 0.054, 0.049, and 0.077 μM under the excitation of 300, 330 and 490 nm, respectively. The utilization of renewable lignin as CQDs-based fluorescent sensors opens a new avenue for the rapid and accurate detection of Cr6+ through a multichannel sensing platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr6+
Lignin
Nitrogen and boron co-doped
Carbon quantum dots
Triple emission

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure of N, B-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and size distribution of N, B-CQDs. (a) TEM images and (b, c) HRTEM images of N, B-CQDs (inset: Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pattern). (c) Size distribution diagram of N, B-CQDs.
Structure characterization. (a) Raman spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra of AL, LNPs, and CQDs.
Structure characterization of AL, LNPs, and CQDs. (a) Survey and high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, (e) B1s XPS spectra of AL, LNPs, and CQDs.

Main substructures identified in the 2D- HSQC NMR spectra of AL, LNPs, and CQDs. (A) β-O-4′ linkages, (B) phenylcoumaran with α-O-4′ and β-O-5′ linkages, (C) resinol with β–β’, α-O-γ’, and γ-O-α’ linkages, (FA) ferulate, (H) p-hydroxyphenyl, (G) guaiacyl units and (G’) oxidized guaiacyl unit bearing a carbonyl group at Cα.
The possible formation mechanism of N, B-CQDs from AL via the two-step route.

Optical properties of N, B-CQDs. (a) Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectra. (b) Detailed PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths in the range from 200 to 700 nm. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra, inset: photographs of N, B-CQDs aqueous solution under different excitation lights (300, 330, and 490 nm). (d) TRPL decay curves at the emission of 346, 428, and 514 nm with the fitted curves.

Selectivity of N, B-CQDs for the detection of Cr6+. (a-c) The fluorescence responses of N, B-CQDs with the addition of various interfering ions (600 μM) under the different excitation wavelengths of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively.

Sensitivity of N, B-CQDs for Cr6+ detection. (a-c) The fluorescence spectra of N, B-CQDs in the presence of the different concentrations of Cr6+ (0–3000 μM). (d-f) The linear fitting curves of (F0-F)/F0 at the different concentrations of Cr6+ in the range of 0–1000 μM and 0–100 μM (insert) excited under 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively.
Illustration for the synthetic route of triple-emission N, B-CQDs.
Highly sensitive Cr6+ detection of N, B-CQDs compared with other reported CQDs, based on the responsive fluorescence quenching.

head.ce:title: Triple-emission nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots from lignin: Highly fluorescent sensing platform for detection of hexavalent chromium ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Thermal Conversion and Control of Ministry of Education, School of Energy and Environment, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Energy Thermal Conversion and Control of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Energy Thermal Conversion and Control of Ministry of Education, School of Energy and Environment, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University College London, London WC1E7JE, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University College London, London WC1E7JE, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering that hexavalent chromium ions (Cr6+) with high toxicity poses a huge threat to human health and the ecological environment, constructing a rapid and accurate sensing platform is of great significance in detecting the toxic substance. The novel nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, B-CQDs) from lignin are synthesized as fluorescent sensors for the detection of Cr6+. The synthetic processes involve the acid hydrolysis step followed by the hydrothermal treatment step. Lignin is firstly depolymerized by cleaving ether bonds in the acidolysis, and N, B-CQDs are consequently formed by the aromatic re-fusion of lignin nanoparticles in the hydrothermal process. The lignin-derived N, B-CQDs show triple emission of purple, blue and green fluorescence under the excitation of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively. The triple-emission N, B-CQDs are applied for the triple-channel detection of Cr6+, which exhibit highly sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching for Cr6+ with good linearity (R2 ≤ 0.996) and very low limit of detection as 0.054, 0.049, and 0.077 μM under the excitation of 300, 330 and 490 nm, respectively. The utilization of renewable lignin as CQDs-based fluorescent sensors opens a new avenue for the rapid and accurate detection of Cr6+ through a multichannel sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is widely distributed in the ecosystem in the form of trivalent chromium ions (Cr3+) and hexavalent chromium ions (Cr6+) [1]. Cr3+ is considered as an indispensable trace element in the human body. However, Cr6+ is an extremely highly toxic heavy metal contaminant, which is ca. 100 times more toxic than Cr3+ [2]. Massive Cr6+ has been discharged into the biological environment from industrial effluents and waste residue, which are produced by stainless steel manufacturing, ore smelting, dyeing, leather tanning, metal electroplating, etc. [3,4]. Excessive Cr6+ is carcinogenic and mutagenic to the respiratory tract, internal organs, and nerve tissue owing to its high oxidation potential and super ability to penetrate biomembranes. It poses a great threat to the environment and human health [5–7]. The maximum allowable limit of Cr6+ in drinking water is 1.92 μM as stipulated by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA), 0.962 μM by the World Health Organization (WHO), and in aquatic products is 2 mg/kg by the National Food Safety Standard of China (GB, 2762–2012) [8,9]. The development of effective Cr6+ detection technology is the top priority to ensure the safety of food and drinking water sources.

The traditional Cr6+ detection technologies include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic emission spectrometry (AES), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and electrochemical detection [10–12]. These technologies show the drawbacks of large or costly equipment, time-consuming preprocessing, complex operation, and tedious analysis, which hinder the rapid detection of Cr6+ on large scale [13]. In comparison, the fluorescent detection method for Cr6+ is considered to overcome these issues for its simple, fast, real-time, and intuitive merits [14–16]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based fluorescent sensors show great potential due to their nontoxicity, good water solubility, rapid response along with adjustable fluorescence characteristics [16]. Wang et al. [6] synthesized bright blue fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs (N, S-CQDs) from citric acid monohydrate and thiosemicarbazide via hydrothermal reaction. The N, S-CQDs-based sensors exhibited sensitive fluorescence response for Cr6+ and toluene with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.33 and 0.03 μM, respectively. The N, S-CQDs-based fluorescent sensors were also obtained by Ji et al. [12] from the rapid stirring of l-cysteine and urea, which showed the LOD as low as 0.02 μM for Cr6+ detection along with 0.28 μM for ascorbic acid detection. Zheng et al. [17] designed controllable functionalization of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) from carbon rods through electrochemical method followed by the solvothermal treatment with ammonia, N, N-dimethylformamide, or polyethylene glycol, respectively. The resultant three kinds N-CQDs were employed as fluorescent sensors for independent detection of Cr6+, ferric ion (Fe3+), and cupric ions (Cu2+). Nevertheless, the abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups on the CQDs surface have a strong affinity for Fe3+ or other interfering metal ions. The CQD-based fluorescent sensors are still largely underdeveloped with the bottleneck in selectivity and reproducibility for Cr6+ detection [18]. In addition, the production of CQDs using toxic organic small-molecule feedstocks increases risks to the environment and human health. The CQDs-based fluorescent sensors derived from biomass waste have great advantages in large-scale production and wide application due to the low cost, renewability, and environmental protection. Herein, there is an urgent need for the development of high-efficient CQDs-based fluorescent sensors from biomass with higher selectivity, greater practicability, and higher accuracy.

In this work, the green synthesis of nitrogen and boron co-doped CQDs (N, B-CQDs) from sustainable lignin via a facile two-step route is proposed for the fluorescent detection of Cr6+. The physicochemical properties of N, B-CQDs are comprehensively characterized and the formation mechanism of N, B-CQDs in the two-step route is excavated. A high-fluorescence tri-channel sensing platform using N, B-CQDs as fluorescent sensors is late established for the rapid and accurate detection of Cr6+. The production of N, B-CQDs-based sensors from lignin provides a green, low-cost, and available strategy for large-scale and efficient detection of Cr6+.

Alkali lignin (AL, 96%) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). 3-Aminophenylboronic acid (≥98%) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals, including sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), sodium fluoride (NaF, 98%), sodium sulfide (Na2S, 99.9%), sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 99%), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, 99.9%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99%), sodium phosphite (NaH2PO2, 99%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%), nickel chloride (NiCl2, 99%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 98%), cupric chloride (CuCl2, 95%), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 96%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98%), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 99%), chromium chloride (CrCl3, 98%), chloride silicon (SiCl4, 99%), aluminum chloride (AlCl3, 99%), cadmium chloride (CdCl2, 99%), mercury chloride (HgCl2, 99.9%), and potassium chromate (K2CrO4, 99.5%) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this experiment. The dialysis bags with a retained molecular weight of 1000 Da were provided by Viskase (USA). The microporous filter membranes of the aqueous system with a diameter of 1.0 and 0.10 μm were obtained by Jinteng (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification.

In a typical two-step synthetic route of N, B-CQDs, 0.3 g of 3-aminophenylboronic acid was added into 30 mL of DI water, and 0.3 g of AL was manually mixed under constant stirring (See Scheme 1 ). The mixed solution was then heated at 90 °C for 1 h in a water bath with constant stirring of 350 rpm. The resultant solution after the reaction was vacuum filtered by a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) microporous membrane (1.0 μm), and filtrate A included the lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) which can be collected by freeze-drying. Meanwhile, the filtrate A was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and kept at a certain temperature for 12 h. Subsequently, the obtained solution was cooled to room temperature and further filtered out by a microporous filter membrane (0.10 μm). The remaining filtrate B was dialyzed in a dialysis bag against DI water for 2 days. The as-dialyzed CQDs aqueous solution was freeze-dried under −60 °C for 2 days and the powders were finally collected for characterization and property measurements. The effects of the different temperatures of 120, 150, 180, 200, and 220 ° C on the lignin-derived CQDs have been investigated.

The detailed morphology was observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). The graphitized structure was recorded by a Micro-Raman spectrometer with InGaN laser excitation at 532 nm (DXR 2xi, ThermoFisher, USA). The chemical composition was characterized by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet Is5, ThermoFisher, USA) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-Alpha, ThermoFisher, USA). The molecular weight was obtained by a matrix-assisted laser desorption/ ionization time of flight mass spectrometer (MALDI-TOF MS, ultrafleXtreme, Bruker, Germany). The 13C–1H chemical environment was measured by the two-dimensional heteronuclear singular quantum correlation nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (2D-HSQC NMR, Avance III 600 MHz, Bruker, Germany) with the dispersant of dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (DMSO) for AL and deuterium oxide (D2O) for LNPs and CQDs. The optical properties were measured by an ultraviolet − visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, UV-5200, Yuanxi, China) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, USA). The time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired from a steady/transient state fluorescence spectrophotometer (TRPL, FLS1000, Edinburgh, UK) equipped with an integrating sphere to evaluate the absolute quantum yields (QYs) of CQDs. The obtained TRPL decay curves were fitted using a multi-exponential function R(t) (Eq. (1)) and the average lifetime (τ avg) was calculated according to Eq. (2) [19,20].
(1)
R
t
=
A 1
e
( - t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e
( - t /
τ 2
)
+
A n
e
( - t /
τ n
)
(2)
τ
avg
=
A 1
τ
1
2
+
A 2
τ
2
2
+
A n
τ
n
2
/
(
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
+
A n
τ n
)
where τ 1, τ 2, τ n represent fluorescence decay lifetimes, τ avg represents the average fluorescence decay lifetime, and A 1, A 2, A n represent pre-exponential factors of τ 1, τ 2, τ n, respectively.

The fluorescent detection of a series of interfering ions was performed at room temperature. 3 mg of N, B-CQDs powders were manually added into 1 L of ion solutions in a concentration of 600 μM, stirred well and then rested for 30 min. Their PL emission spectra were recorded under the excitation of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively. In addition, a series of concentrations of Cr6+ including 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1500, 2000 and 3000 μM were mixed with N, B-CDs aqueous solution, respectively. The corresponding PL emission spectra of these mixed solutions were measured under the excitation of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively. The theoretical LOD was calculated according to Eq. (3) [21]:
(3)
LOD = 3 σ / K
where 3 refers to the signal-to-noise ratio, σ refers to the standard deviation of the blank signal, and K refers to the slope of the linearly concentration-dependent response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of N, B-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The lignin-derived N, B-CQDs are obtained through a mild two-step route involving acid hydrolysis followed by hydrothermal treatment. The hydrothermal temperatures ranging from 120 to 220 ° C are optimized based on the fluorescence QYs of N, B-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. According to Fig.S1, the synthesized N, B-CQDs have unique triple emission, and the QYs especially under 330 and 490 nm excitation are comparable to the reported CQDs derived from other biomass such as rice husk [22], iron ions [23], xylose [24], peanut shell [25], purple perilla [26], bean pod [27], durian shell [28], etc. N, B-CQDs synthesized at 200 ° C show the highest QYs under 300, 330, and 490 nm excitation, respectively, which are selected for further characterization and investigation. The micromorphology of N, B-CQDs can be observed in TEM and HR-TEM images (Fig. 1 a–c). which are well-dispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 3.82 nm (Fig. 1d). The HR-TEM images exhibit one kind of lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm, which represents the (002) crystal plane of graphite [29,30]. The graphitization degrees of AL, LNPs, and CQDs are recorded by Raman spectra (Fig. 2 a). There are two apparent peaks at 1356 cm−1 (D band) and 1572 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to the vibrations of disordered sp3 carbon and graphitized sp2 carbon [31]. The relative intensities (I D/I G) of AL, LNPs, and CQDs, an indicator of the defect density in their structure [32,33], are 1.55, 2.38, and 1.05, respectively. It indicates that more structural defects are introduced in LNPs after the acidolysis of AL while more graphitized structure in CQDs after the hydrothermal reaction.

The chemical compositions and functional groups of AL, LNPs, and CQDs are identified by FT-IR and XPS. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the strong peaks at 3426 cm−1 in FT-IR spectra are assigned to the phenolic hydroxyl group [34], where the stretching vibration is significantly decreased from AL to CQDs. The peaks located at 2936 and 1033 cm−1 are corresponding to in-plane and out-of-plane bending of aromatic CH [35]. The peaks at 1697, 1511/1593, 1264, and 1155 cm−1 are ascribed to CO, aromatic CC, CO, and COC stretching vibration, respectively [36]. Notably, the ether vibration (COC) from AL gradually disappears in LNPs and CQDs, while the new stretching peaks of NH, CN, and B-OH groups emerge at 3354, 1340, and 1000 cm−1 in LNPs and CQDs [18,37]. These demonstrate that CQDs inherit the aromatic skeletons from AL and derive the N, B co-doping from LNPs by acid hydrolysis of AL according to Fig. S2. Meanwhile, plentiful polar functional groups (–OH, NH, CO, CO, and B-OH) attached at the edge of the aromatic skeletons of CQDs suggest its outstanding hydrophilicity [14,31,38]. These results can be further confirmed by XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 3 a show prominent peaks at 168.0, 190.1 284.8, 399.1, and 532.1 eV, which are assigned to S2p, B1s, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [15,18,33,39]. AL is mainly composed of C and O along with a small amount of N and S, while LNPs and CQDs have similar components of C, O, N and B. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s spectra in Fig. 3b can be fitted with three peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.2 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon (CC/CC), sp3 carbon (CO/CN), and carbonyl groups (CO) [40]. There is a sharply decreased CO peak from AL to CQD. The O1s spectra in Fig. 3c can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.8 and 533.2 eV for CO and C OC/C-OH [41], where the latter is decreased from AL to LNPs. This indicates the cleavage of ether bonds by the acidolysis of AL. The N1s spectra in Fig. 3d show two peaks at 399.7 and 401.8 eV for NH and CN groups [42]. The S2p spectra present two peaks at 162.2 and 167.8 eV corresponding to CS and C-SOx groups [32]. The B1s spectra in Fig. 3e can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 190.6 and 192.3 eV corresponding to B-C and B-O groups [42]. Remarkably, there are no significant signals of N1s and B1s of AL, which is consistent with the FT-IR results. The graphitic CN and C-B units newly appear from LNPs to CQDs, inferring that doped N and B are inclined to combine at the edge sites of CQDs along with a small amount of lattice doping. These findings can be supported by the quantitative results of XPS analysis as listed in Table S1. The C content decreases first and then increases while O content is the opposite, implying the depolymerization of AL followed by the re-fusion of LNPs into the aromatic skeletons of CQDs during the synthetic process.

The 2D- HSQC NMR spectra of AL, LNPs, and CQDs are characterized for the in-depth structural insights on the transformation of lignin in the two-step process (Fig. 4 ). The detailed 13C–1H attributions of the 2D-HSQC NMR spectra are listed in Table S2. Lignin is an amorphous polymer with a three-dimensional network structure, which is composed of three types of phenylpropane units connected by ether bonds or CC bonds [43,44]. The HSQC data of AL show plentiful signals in the side-chain region (50–90/3.2–5.0 ppm), assigned to the native structure of lignin including methoxyls (OCH3), β-aryl-ether linkages (A), phenylcoumarane structures (B), and resinol linkages (C) [45]. The signals of ferulate (FA), p-hydroxyphenyl (H), guaiacyl units (G), and oxidized guaiacyl units (G’) are apparently exhibited in the aromatic region (100–135/6.0–7.8 ppm) [46,47]. However, the side-chain signals of A, B, and C structure units are sharply reduced in the HSQC data of LNPs after the acidolysis of AL and almost vanish in the HSQC data of CQDs after the hydrothermal treatment of LNPs. It indicates that AL is strongly de-etherified via the cleavage of A, B, and C linkages in the two-step synthesis process, which is consistent with the results of the above Raman, FT-IR and XPS. Meanwhile, the N and B co-doping evidenced by FT-IR and XPS results may contribute to abundant active sites exposed on the surface of LNPs for further transformation. The aromatic structures (FA, H, G, and G’) of AL are well inherited by LNPs and CQDs during the synthesis process.

According to the experimental evidences above, the possible formation mechanism of N, B-CQDs from AL via the two-step route is proposed as follows (Fig. 5 ): In the 1st step of acid hydrolysis, AL is de-ether decomposed into LNPs through the catalytic cleavage of ether linkages, with simultaneous N, B co-doping via covalent conjugation [38]. Thereby, various active sites including oxygen-, nitrogen- and boron- functional groups can be generated on the LNPs surface. Notably, 3-aminophenylboronic acid plays a triple role of “top-down” scissor, N, B dopant as well as a surfactant during the acidolysis process [14,48]. In the 2nd step, the functionalized LNPs serve as precursors for the preparation of CQDs. The dehydration condensation of activated side chains on LNPs results in the prompt π-π stacking and the increased sp2 hybridization under the hydrothermal treatment [32,49]. The CN and C–B groups are simultaneously enhanced in the carbonization process, eventually resulting in the formation of CQDs [50]. In summary, the formation of N, B co-doped CQDs involves the acid dissociation of AL first followed by the aromatic re-fusion of LNPs.

The optical properties of fluorescent N, B-CQDs are further analyzed. The PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths from 200 to 700 nm are displayed in Fig. 6 a and 6b. There are three distinct PL centers located at (300, 346), (330, 428), and (490, 514) nm, corresponding to the emission of purple, blue and green fluorescence, respectively. This indicates that the N, B-CQDs prepared from AL have unique triple-emission fluorescence, which is rarely found in the previously reported biomass-derived CQDs [51,52]. According to Fig. S3a and S3c, N, B-CQDs exhibit almost excitation-independent property under the excitation of 230–300 nm and 440–490, which indicates the fluorescence emission may originate from fewer surface defects [42,53,54]. Oppositely, the emission peaks are gradually red-shifted from 350 to 496 nm when the excitation wavelengths increase from 310 to 430 nm with a 10 nm increment (Fig. S3b). This pronounced excitation-dependent behavior is associated with diverse surface defects introduced by O, N, or B functional groups [42,48]. As for the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6c), N, B-CQDs show two strong adsorption peaks at 230, 281 nm and a weak one at 343 nm, with an optical absorption edge at ∼500 nm. The two peaks below 300 nm are attributed to the π–π* transition of CC in the carbon core, while the other over 300 nm are corresponding to the n–π* transition of CO/CN on the CQDs surface [53,55]. Their corresponding absorbance intensities depend on the conjugated degree in the carbon core and defects state on the surface, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 6d, the TRPL spectra of the triple-emission N, B-CQDs are monitored at excitation wavelengths of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively. The three decay curves can be biexponentially fitted at the excitation of 300 and 330 nm while monoexponentially fitted at 490 nm (the fitting parameters are summarized in Table S3). The τ avg of N, B-CQDs at 300, 330, and 490 nm excitation are 1.26, 3.91, and 3.41 ns, respectively, which are consistent with the variation trend of QYs. The biexponential lifetimes denote two PL centers including a fast component (τ 1) originated from the radiative recombination of intrinsic state (π-π*) as well as a slow component (τ 2) ascribed to the non-radiative process of surface state (n-π*) [39,56]. The proportions of τ 1 excited at 300 and 330 nm of the N, B-CQDs are 81.55% and 75.93%, which demonstrate that their PL emissions are mainly attributed to the sp2 conjugated structure in the carbon core. The proportion of τ 2 excited at 330 nm increases to 24.07% compared with that at 300 nm, corresponding to the enhanced effect of surface defects on PL emission. The PL emissions of N, B-CQDs excited at 490 nm present a monoexponential lifetime, implying a single PL center of molecular state [57,58]. The difference concerning PL centers at 300, 330, and 490 nm excitation further explains the triple-emission performance of N, B-CQDs. As well known, the O, N, and B-related functional groups may serve as electron acceptor and surface-passivation agents to induce strong intramolecular charge transfer and improved emission efficiency, respectively. The O-related functional groups, especially CO with the strong electron-accepting property are considered to be chromophores. The N, B related groups are identified as auxochrome groups, the isolated n electrons of which and π electrons of the carbon ring are almost coplanar. The generated defect states lead to the additional energy levels (n-π* transitions) between π and π* transition [59]. The additional n-π* transitions result in multiple radiation recombination paths back to the ground state, thereby exhibiting unique PL performances [60].

In view of the green synthesis, low toxicity, good water solubility, and unique optical properties, the selectivity and sensitivity of N, B-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cr6+ are investigated to estimate its sensing performance. The selectivity of N, B-CQDs towards Cr6+ and other interfering ions is evaluated, including the anions of Cl-, F-, S2-, NO3 –, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, H2PO2 - and metal cations of Na+, K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Si3+, Al3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Cr6+. As presented in Fig. 7 a-7c, the PL intensities of N, B-CQDs aqueous solution at different excitations all show a slight decrease in the presence of Fe3+and maintain well in the presence of other interfering ions (600 μM). However, there is a significant fluorescence quenching in terms of Cr6+ under 300, 330, and 490 nm excitation, respectively. Compared to other CQDs-based sensors that also have a fluorescence quenching effect on Fe3+, this N, B-CQDs-based sensor exhibits the superiority in selectivity towards Cr6+. The average lifetimes are reduced from 1.3 to 1.1 ns, 3.9 to 3.6 ns, and 3.4 to 3.0 ns under the excitation wavelengths of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively (Fig. S4). The fluorescence quenching induced by Cr6+ is considered to be originated from the dynamic quenching dominated by collisional deactivation [5,15,17,18]. UV–Vis spectra of Cr6+, N, B-CQDs, and N, B-CQDs in the presence of Cr6+ are shown in Fig. S5. There are two strong absorption peaks around 270 and 368 nm, which are overlapped with those of N, B-CQDs. It may suppress electron transitions and cause an extinction effect on the fluorescence emission of N, B-CQDs [10]. In addition, N, B-CQDs are decorated with –NH2, -BOH, –COOH, and –OH surface groups as electron donors, and Cr6+ has a stronger binding affinity than other interfering ions towards the electron-donating groups. It can induce considerable energy dissipation instead by more rapid electron transfer and restrain recombination of excitons, thereby sharper fluorescence reduction of N, B-CQDs [14,18].

The novel N, B-CQDs for the detection of Cr6+ are synthesized from renewable lignin by a green two-step route, which involves the de-etherization and depolymerization of AL in the acidolysis and followed by the aromatic re-fusion of LNPs in the hydrothermal process. The lignin-derived N, B-CQDs exhibit unique triple emission of purple, blue and green fluorescence under the excitation wavelengths of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively. Compared with the reported CQDs-based fluorescent sensors [10,17,18,62,63], the triple-emission N, B-CQDs-based sensor in this work presents the higher selectivity and sensitivity for Cr6+ detection, with the lower LOD as 0.054, 0.049, and 0.077 μM under the excitation of 300, 330 and 490 nm, respectively. The fluorescence quenching of Cr6+ detected by N, B-CQDs can be attributed to the dynamic fluorescence quenching caused by electron-donating amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the CQDs surface. Therefore, the lignin-derived N, B-CQDs can serve as a promising candidate for the rapid, visualized, and accurate detection of Cr6+ through triple channel in aqueous media.

Lingli Zhu: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Validation. Dekui Shen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Kai Hong Luo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Dekui Shen reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Dekui Shen reports financial support was provided by Key Research & Development Program of Jiangsu Province. Dekui Shen reports financial support was provided by Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province from the Education Department of Jiangsu.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers. 51676047 and 51861145102) and the Key Research & Development Program of Jiangsu Province (grant number: BE2020114). The authors also acknowledge the funding support from the Scientific Research Foundation of Graduate School of Southeast University, China (YBPY2109) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province from the Education Department of Jiangsu (KYCX21_0094).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.03.039.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid detection of Cr6+ ions based on cobalt2+-doped carbon dots
